1: weak sense of purpose; the person drifts with the current. They long for a better life, yet never turn that longing into an actual goal. They rely on the people around them. And when life does not work out, they blame anyone but themselves. The drive is faint; such a person may jump into an argument, but that does not mean they truly want to reach a result — as a rule, it is only the urge to defeat the other in the dispute. They place their hopes on chance and on friends.
2: this is the norm, yet the person moves toward their goals slowly. First they set a goal, then they assess their own capabilities, adjust the goal, and only after that begin to advance toward it — slowly and hesitantly. Such a person takes a long run-up through life. They first learn what they are capable of, and only then begin to set themselves worthy goals.
3: a normal sense of purpose, yet the person changes their goals quickly and chaotically. Their choices have no real justification and depend entirely on mood. The person is forever caught up in endless preparation and secondary tasks, pushing the achievement of results away with their own hands. They wind along as if on a mountain serpentine.
4: a strong sense of purpose. The person sets a goal and reaches it quickly. But often they achieve goals that are not truly their own. The goals need to be tested for authenticity. Were they imposed from outside? Is this truly the goal the person wants?
5: a strong sense of purpose. The person presses toward the goal, forgetting about family and loved ones. A sense of measure must be kept in check. Such a person can set goals and race toward them, forgetting about everything around them.
6: critical overload. These people are highly ambitious and set themselves many goals. They want everything at once, take on several projects simultaneously, grab at anything and everything, and as a result scatter their energy without reaching a result in any of it.
7: critical overload. These people are highly ambitious and set themselves many goals. They want everything at once, take on several projects simultaneously, grab at anything and everything, and as a result scatter their energy without reaching a result in any of it.

The drive is faint; such a person may jump into an argument, but that does not mean they truly want to reach a result — as a rule, it is only the urge to defeat the other in the dispute.
